unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 7 of your Year 8 English course. In this unit, you will explore the theme of Historical Figures and Legacy -- a topic that invites you to think about how individuals shape history and how their actions echo through generations. You will read a powerful story set in 1948 East Africa, learn essential grammar structures for narrating past events, and build a rich vocabulary for discussing argument, leadership, emotion, and change.
unit.learningPath: Preview the goals and vocabulary. Read and annotate the source texts. Compare evidence, viewpoint and source reliability. Apply the grammar focus. Draft and revise a cohesive multi-paragraph response. Present, defend, reflect and complete the quiz.

visual.alt: Learners interpreting historical figures, leadership and legacy

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Kind and gentle; not harmful or dangerous.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The teacher's benign smile made the students feel comfortable in class.
practiceSentences: The teacher's benign smile made the students feel comfortable in class.
The word 1. benign belongs to our vocabulary group 1 vocabulary.
I can explain 1. benign in my own words.

readings.title: Historical Figures and Legacy: source text 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Welcome to Unit 7 of your Year 8 English course. In this unit, you will explore the theme of Historical Figures and Legacy -- a topic that invites you to think about how individuals shape history and how their actions echo through generations. You will read a powerful story set in 1948 East Africa, learn essential grammar structures for narrating past events, and build a rich vocabulary for discussing argument, leadership, emotion, and change.
The centrepiece of this unit is an original story called "The Rebellion of Silence." It follows a quiet but determined student named Isaac at a fictional East African boarding school during the late colonial period. Through Isaac's journey, you will see how silence can be a form of protest and how one person's initiative can change the tone of an entire community. The story raises questions about obedience, courage, freedom of expression, and what it truly means to leave a legacy.
Throughout this unit, you will practise telling stories about the past using Past Simple and Past Continuous tenses. You will also study Past Perfect, Reported Speech, Verbs Followed by Prepositions, Phrasal Verbs, Reflexive Pronouns, Time Phrases, and Discourse Markers. These grammar tools will help you narrate events clearly, report what others have said, and connect your ideas in both speech and writing.
This unit is designed for self-paced learning. You will work through each section at your own speed, with support from your AI Tutor available around the clock and live teacher sessions twice a week. Every section includes activities, reflection questions, and practice exercises to keep you engaged and help you track your progress.
Read through each section carefully at your own pace.
Complete all activities and exercises in your notebook or on screen.
Use the Ask Your AI Tutor prompts whenever you need help or want to go deeper.
Attend your twice-weekly teacher sessions to ask questions and practise speaking.
Check your understanding with the Self-Assessment at the end of the unit.
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Use the Past Simple and Past Continuous tenses correctly to narrate events and describe actions in progress in the past.
Form and use the Past Perfect tense to show which action happened first when telling a story.
Convert direct speech into Reported Speech, adjusting pronouns, verb tenses, and word order correctly.
Identify and use verbs followed by prepositions such as insist on, believe in, waver between, and implore for.
Recognise and use common phrasal verbs including get on with, get into trouble, ebb away, and roil over.
Use reflexive pronouns (myself, yourself, himself, herself, themselves, etc.) correctly in sentences.
Employ time phrases such as in those days, eventually, at the time, and by 1948 to signal when events happened.
Use discourse markers such as well, basically, for example, in fact, and actually to connect ideas smoothly.
Read and analyse a historical fiction story set in East Africa, identifying themes of legacy, protest, and leadership.
Expand your vocabulary with over 30 new words related to argument, emotion, historical reasoning, and character.
Write a short narrative essay using past tenses, reported speech, and new vocabulary.
Speak confidently about historical figures, personal heroes, and the concept of legacy.
Before you begin reading the story, it is important to become familiar with the key vocabulary you will encounter. This unit introduces 33 words that relate to argument, emotion, character, and historical settings. Take a few minutes now to scan the list below. You do not need to memorise every word immediately -- you will meet them again in the story, grammar exercises, and vocabulary activities later in the unit.
Here are all 33 words for this unit, grouped by theme to help you see how they connect.
You will find a complete vocabulary reference with example sentences in the separate Vocabulary document for this unit. Return to that document whenever you need a reminder of what a word means or how to use it in a sentence.

readings.title: Historical Figures and Legacy: source text 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
This unit covers several grammar topics that all work together to help you tell stories about the past. Here is a brief overview of what you will learn. Each topic is covered in full detail in the separate Grammar document, but this introduction will give you a clear starting point.
The Past Simple tense describes actions that happened and finished in the past. You form it by adding -ed to regular verbs (played, walked, finished) or by using the special form for irregular verbs (went, wrote, found). The Past Continuous tense describes actions that were in progress at a particular time in the past. You form it with was/were + verb-ing (was writing, were playing).
When you combine these two tenses, you can show that one action interrupted another, or that two actions were happening at the same time. For example: "Isaac was writing an essay when the prefect found his notebook." Here, the Past Continuous (was writing) shows the ongoing action, and the Past Simple (found) shows the action that interrupted it.
This combination is essential for storytelling. In "The Rebellion of Silence," you will see many examples of these tenses working together to create a vivid picture of events unfolding in time.
The Past Perfect tense helps you show which action happened first when you are describing two past events. You form it with had + past participle (had finished, had written, had seen). For example: "By the time the headmaster arrived, Isaac had already hidden his notebook." The Past Perfect makes the order of events crystal clear.
Reported Speech (also called indirect speech) is what you use when you tell someone what another person said, without quoting their exact words. You change the pronouns, shift the verb tense back, and adjust time expressions. For example, if Noah said, "We need to keep this going," you would report it as: Noah said that they needed to keep that going. You will practise converting direct speech into reported speech throughout this unit.
Some English verbs must be followed by a specific preposition to make sense. For this unit, the key combinations are: insist on, believe in, waver between, and implore for. Getting the preposition right is important because using the wrong one can change the meaning or make the sentence sound unnatural.
Phrasal verbs combine a verb with a preposition or adverb to create a new meaning. In this unit, you will learn: get on with (to continue doing something), get into trouble (to find yourself in difficulty), ebb away (to gradually decrease), and roil over (to become disturbed or agitated). These are common in everyday English and appear naturally in the story.
Reflexive pronouns are used when the subject and object of a sentence are the same person. The forms are: myself, yourself, himself, herself, itself, ourselves, yourselves, themselves. For example: "Isaac taught himself to write persuasive essays." Reflexive pronouns add a personal touch to your writing and help you express self-directed actions.
Time phrases like in those days, eventually, at the time, and by 1948 help you signal when events took place. Discourse markers like well, basically, actually, in fact, and for example help you connect your ideas and guide your reader or listener through your argument. Both are essential for smooth, natural storytelling.
The story for this unit is called "The Rebellion of Silence." It is set in 1948 at a fictional East African boarding school called Ibrahim Boys' School, in the hills of northern Tanganyika (modern-day Tanzania). The story follows Isaac, a quiet but thoughtful student who uses the power of writing to challenge an unjust rule banning debate and political discussion on campus.
You will find the complete story in the separate Story document for this unit. Before you read, take a moment to prepare yourself with the pre-reading activities below.
Think about the following questions before you begin reading. You do not need to write answers now, but let them guide your thinking as you read.

readings.title: The Rebellion of Silence, part 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Theme: Voice, leadership, and ideological change in a tense political setting
Setting: A fictional East African boarding school in 1948
Genre: Historical fiction with emotional depth and political debate
In 1948, the stone paths of Ibrahim Boys' School twisted between mango trees and unsightly dormitory blocks. The buildings bore the wear of time -- peeling paint, rusted windows -- but the minds inside them were ecstatic with questions.
Among the Form 4 students was Isaac, a boy with a quiet disposition and a habit of observing more than speaking. He kept a leather notebook in his pocket, filled with phrases, speeches, and small essays scribbled in the margins. His teachers called him bright. His classmates called him strange. But most saw him as harmless -- a benign shadow in the back row.
Headmaster Adam, a stiff British man with a stiff red nose, summoned the students to the courtyard. His voice boomed: "Starting next week, debate and politics will be forbidden on school grounds."
There was a long pause. Then murmurs.
"For those who do not comply," Adam added, "we will have no choice but to banish them."
Isaac's hand tightened around his notebook.
By 1948, whispers of independence had begun to drift across the continent like wind across long grass. Radios crackled with stories from Nairobi, Accra, and Dar es Salaam. But here, in the hills of northern Tanganyika, silence was still enforced -- through rules, expectations, and fear.
The students were instructed to focus on their studies and not "get into trouble." One evening, while others played football, Isaac slipped behind the old assembly hall. There, he found Noah, his classmate, and three others gathered around a candle. They were sharing a cache of newspapers and pamphlets hidden in a biscuit tin.
"We need to keep this going," Noah whispered. "Debate, writing, everything. We can't let them take our voices."
Isaac wavered. "But what if they catch us?"
Actually, they could. And they had before. But something in Noah's voice kindled a flame inside Isaac -- something bold, profound.
He began crafting essays at night, under the blankets. One was titled The Burden of Obedience. Another: When Laws Become Shackles. His tone was cautious but emphatic, each sentence carefully justified. He used phrases like "In those days" and "Eventually, the silence will end."
He submitted the first anonymously, sliding it under the common room door where staff sometimes found lost items. To his surprise, it was read aloud during morning literature class.
"This is... daring," said Miss Sarah, the Kiswahili teacher. She raised an eyebrow but said nothing more.
Word spread. Students began slipping quotes into conversation. Debates re-emerged -- furtive but fierce. Ibrahim's silence was ebbing.
Then, during a Saturday dorm inspection, a prefect found Isaac's notebook hidden behind a loose brick. It was taken straight to the headmaster.
"You wrote this?" Adam asked the next morning, waving a sheet with red annotations.
Isaac nodded, trying not to waver.
Adam sighed. "You've caused... unrest. And now you'll replenish it with order."
He assigned Isaac to write a formal apology and clean every classroom for a week.
That week was strenuous. By midday each day, Isaac's arms ached. Still, he noticed things -- how quickly silence returned, how quickly fear was implored.
Noah was furious. "You should have resisted! Why didn't you make a scene?"
"I did what I had to," Isaac replied. "Resistance isn't always noise."
"Well," Noah muttered, "your quiet rebellion isn't working."
Two days later, another essay appeared -- this time by someone else. Then another. And another. They were printed by hand, passed under desks. A system had formed. It was systematic.
Part Six: Cracks in the Wall
One night, Adam addressed the students.
"I know some of you believe in change. But this school is not a parliament. It is a place of learning."

readings.title: The Rebellion of Silence, part 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
He implored them to focus on books, not battles.
But even as he spoke, the walls of silence were starting to crack.
At the term's end, the school hosted a speech contest. Adam considered canceling it, but the board insisted. "It teaches confidence," they said.
Students submitted their topics in sealed envelopes. Isaac's title was simple: On the Edge of Thought.
When he stood to speak, the hall quieted.
"In those days," he began, "we believed that silence was peace. That if we obeyed, we would be safe."
He paused. "But silence is not always peace. It is sometimes the defect in freedom."
He went on, telling the story -- not by name, but clearly -- of what had happened. He ended with a quote:
The hall clapped. Adam did not.
By the next week, Isaac had been called to the office again. The board was debating whether to banish him.
Adam stared. "Do you justify your actions?"
Isaac said, "I believe in learning. I believe in questions. If that's a fault, then I'm guilty."
"You're lucky the board is more liberal than I am."
Isaac left, not sure if he'd been punished or protected.
Weeks passed. The rainy season brought mud to the courtyards and dampness to the dorms. But it also brought a new initiative: student-run discussion clubs, approved by the board under strict guidelines. Adam remained skeptical, but he had lost his ability to roil the student body into submission.
Isaac was invited to help organise the first club meeting. He declined at first, but Noah insisted.
"You started this. You should speak."
"Well," Isaac said slowly, "maybe I'll attend."
The meeting was held in the old library, a room that had once been used for storage. Now, swept clean and lined with borrowed stools, it pulsed with energy.
Topics ranged from the circumference of social justice to the defects in colonial education systems. Some discussions were clumsy. Others, emphatic. Isaac noticed students who had never raised a hand in class were suddenly leading arguments.
Even the head prefect -- a strict, conservative student named Selma -- joined the second meeting.
"I don't agree with all this liberal chatter," she said. "But I respect the courage it takes to speak."
Her presence changed everything. It gave the movement weight, credibility.
One afternoon, Isaac was called to Miss Sarah's office. She greeted him with tea.
"You've done something rare," she said.
"I got in trouble," Isaac replied.
"You changed the tone of the school."
He smiled, then frowned. "But what happens after I leave?"
"That depends on who follows you."
Years later, Isaac would return to Ibrahim as a speaker. He would walk past the old dormitories, past the mango trees, and into the new auditorium named after Miss Sarah.
A portrait of her hung near the door. The caption read: She believed in brave breath.
He stepped onto the stage. The room was packed. Students from every year sat shoulder to shoulder, eyes bright, hands full of notebooks.
"In those days," he began, "silence was safe. But safety is not the same as freedom. And freedom does not always shout. Sometimes it waits. It listens. And then it speaks."
The following vocabulary words from Unit 7 appear in this story:
benign, reprieve, defect, disposition, systematic, cache, strenuous, conclusion, waver, ebb
replenish, exasperate, petulant, banish, conservative, emphatic, profound, counterargument
implore, ecstatic, justify, connoisseur, abate, circumference, barter, roil, habitation
As you re-read the story, look for examples of:
Past Simple vs Past Continuous (e.g., "while others played football, Isaac slipped behind the hall")
Past Perfect (e.g., "whispers of independence had begun to drift across the continent")
Reported Speech (e.g., Adam's announcements and commands)
Verbs + Prepositions (e.g., believe in, insist on, waver between)
Phrasal Verbs (e.g., get into trouble, get on with, ebb away)
Reflexive Pronouns and Time Phrases (e.g., "In those days," "By 1948")

grammar.title: How to Form Them
grammar.explanation:
Past Simple -- Regular Verbs: Add -ed to the base form of the verb.
play --> played
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Past Simple -- Regular Verbs: Add -ed to the base form of the verb.
play --> played
walk --> walked
finish --> finished
Past Simple -- Irregular Verbs: These have special forms that you need to memorise.
go --> went
write --> wrote
find --> found
have --> had
see --> saw
Past Continuous: Use was (I/he/she/it) or were (you/we/they) + the verb with -ing.
I was writing
They were playing
She was reading
When to Use Each Tense
Use the Past Simple when you want to describe a completed action in the past:
"She led a protest." (The protest happened and finished.)
"He found the notebook." (The finding happened at a specific moment.)
Use the Past Continuous when you want to describe an action that was in progress at a specific time:
"She was leading a protest at noon." (The leading was ongoing at that time.)
"They were studying when the bell rang." (The studying was ongoing when the bell interrupted.)
Combining the Two Tenses
When you combine these tenses in one sentence, you show that one action was happening (Past Continuous) when another action interrupted it or happened during it (Past Simple).
"Isaac was writing an essay when the prefect found his notebook."
"While the students were playing football, Isaac slipped behind the assembly hall."
"She was reading the essay aloud when she realised its importance."
You can also use the Past Continuous twice to show two actions happening at the same time:
"While she was leading the discussion, others were bartering ideas."
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply how to form them in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Common Mistakes and How to Fix Them
grammar.explanation:
Mistake 1: Using the wrong verb form.
Wrong: "She lead a protest."
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Mistake 1: Using the wrong verb form.
Wrong: "She lead a protest."
Correct: "She led a protest." (Led is the past form of lead.)
Mistake 2: Confusing which tense to use.
Wrong: "While she led a protest, others barter supplies."
Correct: "While she was leading a protest, others were bartering supplies."
Memory Tip
Think about timing. If an action is completed and done, use the Past Simple (like finishing your homework). If an action was happening over time in the background, use the Past Continuous (like painting a picture while music was playing).
Practice Exercise
Complete each sentence using either the Past Simple or Past Continuous form of the verb in brackets.
She __________ (lead) a protest while others __________ (barter) supplies.
They __________ (play) outside when it __________ (start) to rain.
I __________ (watch) a movie when my sister __________ (call) me.
While the headmaster __________ (speak), the students __________ (whisper) to each other.
Isaac __________ (write) in his notebook when the candle __________ (go) out.
Answers:
led; were bartering
were playing; started
was watching; called
was speaking; were whispering
was writing; went
2. Past Perfect
What the Past Perfect Means and Why It Matters
The Past Perfect tense is used to talk about an action that was completed before another action in the past. It helps you show the order of events clearly. When you use the Past Perfect, your reader or listener can immediately understand which action happened first.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply common mistakes and how to fix them in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: How to Form It
grammar.explanation:
The Past Perfect is formed with had + the past participle of the main verb.
Regular verbs: had + -ed form (had played, had walked, had finished)
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
The Past Perfect is formed with had + the past participle of the main verb.
Regular verbs: had + -ed form (had played, had walked, had finished)
Irregular verbs: had + special past participle (had gone, had written, had seen, had eaten)
Structure: Subject + had + past participle + rest of sentence
When to Use It
Use the Past Perfect when you want to make it clear that one past action happened before another past action.
"He had concluded the speech before the defect was revealed."
"She had finished her homework before dinner started."
"They had left the party when the rain began."
"By the time the headmaster arrived, Isaac had already hidden his notebook."
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply how to form it in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Common Mistakes and How to Fix Them
grammar.explanation:
Mistake: Using the Past Simple instead of the Past Perfect when talking about the earlier action.
Wrong: "He concluded the speech before the defect was revealed." (This implies both happened at roughly the same time.)
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Mistake: Using the Past Simple instead of the Past Perfect when talking about the earlier action.
Wrong: "He concluded the speech before the defect was revealed." (This implies both happened at roughly the same time.)
Correct: "He had concluded the speech before the defect was revealed." (This clearly shows the speech was finished first.)
Memory Tip
Think of the Past Perfect as a time traveller. It goes further back in time to show what happened first. When you see "had," it is helping you tell the story in the right order.
Practice Exercise
Complete each sentence using the Past Perfect form of the verb in brackets.
By the time she arrived, I __________ (finish) my book.
They __________ (see) the movie before it was released in cinemas.
He __________ (eat) all the biscuits before his friends came over.
By the time the bell rang, the students __________ (complete) the test.
She __________ (leave) the room before the announcement was made.
Answers:
had finished
had seen
had eaten
had completed
had left
3. Reported Speech
What Reported Speech Is and Why It Matters
Reported speech (also called indirect speech) is when you tell someone what another person said without using their exact words. Instead of quoting them directly, you adjust the pronouns, verb tenses, and sometimes the time expressions. Reported speech is essential for sharing information, retelling conversations, and writing about what people have said.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply common mistakes and how to fix them in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: How to Convert Direct Speech to Reported Speech
grammar.explanation:
Follow these steps:
Identify the speaker and the original statement. Example: She said, "The response is emphatic."
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Follow these steps:
Identify the speaker and the original statement. Example: She said, "The response is emphatic."
Change the pronouns to match who is being talked about. "I" might become "he" or "she."
Shift the verb tense back one step. Present becomes past; past becomes past perfect.
Add "that" (optional) to connect the reporting verb to the reported statement.
Put it all together. Example: She said that the response was emphatic.
Tense Shift Table
Reporting Questions and Commands
Questions: Use "asked" + if/whether (for yes/no questions) or the question word (for wh- questions). Do not use a question mark in reported speech.
Direct: He asked, "Are you coming?"
Reported: He asked if I was coming.
Direct: She asked, "Where are you going?"
Reported: She asked where I was going.
Commands: Use "told" + person + to + base verb.
Direct: He said, "Justify your actions."
Reported: He told them to justify their actions.
Direct: She said, "Please close the door."
Reported: She asked me to close the door.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply how to convert direct speech to reported speech in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Common Mistakes and How to Fix Them
grammar.explanation:
Mistake 1: Forgetting to shift the verb tense.
Wrong: She said the response is emphatic.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Mistake 1: Forgetting to shift the verb tense.
Wrong: She said the response is emphatic.
Correct: She said that the response was emphatic.
Mistake 2: Not changing questions properly.
Wrong: He asked are you happy.
Correct: He asked if I was happy.
Memory Tip
Think of reported speech as a time traveller: when you report what someone said, you travel back in time, so the verb tense shifts one step into the past.
Practice Exercise
Change each sentence from direct speech to reported speech.
She said, "The game is fun."
They asked, "Where are you going?"
He told her, "Finish your homework."
Isaac said, "I believe in questions."
Adam asked, "Did you write this?"
Answers:
She said that the game was fun.
They asked where I was going.
He told her to finish her homework.
Isaac said that he believed in questions.
Adam asked whether he had written that.
4. Verbs Followed by Prepositions
What This Rule Means and Why It Matters
In English, some verbs must be followed by a specific preposition to make the sentence correct and natural. Using the wrong preposition -- or leaving it out entirely -- can change the meaning or make the sentence sound unnatural. Learning which prepositions go with which verbs is an important part of building fluent English.
Key Verb + Preposition Combinations
Other Common Verb + Preposition Pairs
look at = to see something / look for = to search for something
depend on = to rely on something or someone
listen to = to pay attention to sounds or someone speaking
agree with = to have the same opinion as someone
belong to = to be owned by someone
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply common mistakes and how to fix them in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

speaking.title: Speaking 1: Story Retelling
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Retell the story of "The Rebellion of Silence" in your own words. Try to include at least five vocabulary words from the unit and use a mix of Past Simple and Past Continuous tenses. Aim to speak for two to three minutes without stopping. If you are working with a partner, take turns retelling different sections of the story.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise story retelling and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary, evidence, reasoning and audience effect.

speaking.title: Speaking 2: Discussion Questions
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Discuss the following questions with a partner, in a small group, or with your AI Tutor. Give full answers and support your opinions with reasons.
Who is someone you consider a historical figure? It does not have to be someone famous -- it could be a grandparent, a community leader, or a teacher. Why do you admire them?
Do you think students should be allowed to discuss politics at school? Why or why not?
What does "legacy" mean to you? What kind of legacy would you like to leave behind?
In the story, Isaac says, "Speech is breath made brave." What does this mean to you?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise discussion questions and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary, evidence, reasoning and audience effect.

speaking.title: Speaking 3: Reported Speech Role-Play
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Work with a partner. One person plays the role of a character from the story (Isaac, Noah, Adam, Miss Sarah, or Selma) and says something in direct speech. The other person reports what they said using reported speech. Then swap roles. Here are some example starting lines:
Isaac: "I believe in learning and questions."
Adam: "Debate and politics will be forbidden on school grounds."
Noah: "We need to keep this going."
Miss Sarah: "You have done something rare."
Samira: "I do not agree with all this liberal chatter, but I respect the courage it takes to speak."
After your partner speaks, report what they said. For example: Isaac said that he believed in learning and questions.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise reported speech role-play and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary, evidence, reasoning and audience effect.

speaking.title: Speaking 4: Mini-Debate
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Choose one of the following topics and prepare a short argument (one to two minutes). Use discourse markers (well, basically, in fact, actually, for example) to connect your ideas. Then present your argument to a partner or your AI Tutor.
Silence is a more powerful form of protest than shouting.
Young people have the right to discuss politics at school.
One person can change the direction of a whole community.
It is better to follow rules even when you disagree with them.
Ask Your AI Tutor
Try asking: Can you be my debate partner? I want to argue that silence is a powerful form of protest. Please argue the other side.
Writing Practice
Writing allows you to bring together everything you have learned -- vocabulary, grammar, ideas, and structure. The activities below will guide you through different types of writing, from short exercises to a full narrative essay.
Activity 1: Sentence Transformation
Rewrite the following sentences using the grammar structure indicated in brackets. Write your answers in your notebook.
"I am happy with my essay," said Isaac. (Change to reported speech.)
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise mini-debate and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary, evidence, reasoning and audience effect.

speaking.title: Speaking 5: Vocabulary Expert Interview
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Choose six unit words. Ask and answer a clear question about each word using complete sentences.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise vocabulary expert interview and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary, evidence, reasoning and audience effect.

speaking.title: Speaking 6: Explain and Respond
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Explain one important unit idea, give a reason with because, and respond to two follow-up questions.
Develop this into a sustained five- to six-minute response with synthesized evidence, source evaluation, a counterargument and answers to follow-up questions.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise explain and respond and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary, evidence, reasoning and audience effect.

writing.title: Writing 1:
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Before you submit your essay, check the following:
Have you used at least five vocabulary words from this unit?
Have you used the Past Simple and Past Continuous correctly?
Have you included at least one example of the Past Perfect?
Have you used at least one example of reported speech?
Have you included time phrases and discourse markers to connect your ideas?
Have you checked your spelling and punctuation?
Does your essay have a clear beginning, middle, and end?
Ask Your AI Tutor
Try asking: Can you read my essay and give me feedback on my use of past tenses and vocabulary? Here is what I wrote...
Activities and Exercises
This section brings together grammar, vocabulary, and reading comprehension in a series of varied exercises. Work through each one carefully. The answers are provided at the end of this section so you can check your work.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 8 response sustains a line of thought across cohesive paragraphs, integrates precise vocabulary and evidence, evaluates sources and shapes language for audience and purpose.
writing.sentenceStarter: The evidence supports the view that...
writing.successCriteria: I sustained a clear line of thought; shaped writing for audience and purpose; integrated precise vocabulary and attributed evidence; compared viewpoints and source reliability; used cohesive devices and varied structures; revised content and accuracy
writing.support: Plan a thesis or controlling idea, paragraph sequence, evidence and source notes before drafting.
writing.extension: Synthesize three sources, qualify claims, address and rebut a counterargument, and refine style for deliberate effect.

writing.successCriteria: I sustained a clear line of thought; shaped writing for audience and purpose; integrated precise vocabulary and attributed evidence; compared viewpoints and source reliability; used cohesive devices and varied structures; revised content and accuracy
writing.support: Plan a thesis or controlling idea, paragraph sequence, evidence and source notes before drafting.
writing.extension: Synthesize three sources, qualify claims, address and rebut a counterargument, and refine style for deliberate effect.

activities.title: Activities 1: Story Retelling
activities.instructionsAndItems: Retell the story of "The Rebellion of Silence" in your own words. Try to include at least five vocabulary words from the unit and use a mix of Past Simple and Past Continuous tenses. Aim to speak for two to three minutes without stopping. If you are working with a partner, take turns retelling different sections of the story.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 2: Discussion Questions
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Discuss the following questions with a partner, in a small group, or with your AI Tutor. Give full answers and support your opinions with reasons.
Who is someone you consider a historical figure? It does not have to be someone famous -- it could be a grandparent, a community leader, or a teacher. Why do you admire them?
Do you think students should be allowed to discuss politics at school? Why or why not?
What does "legacy" mean to you? What kind of legacy would you like to leave behind?
In the story, Isaac says, "Speech is breath made brave." What does this mean to you?
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 3: Reported Speech Role-Play
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Work with a partner. One person plays the role of a character from the story (Isaac, Noah, Adam, Miss Sarah, or Selma) and says something in direct speech. The other person reports what they said using reported speech. Then swap roles. Here are some example starting lines:
Isaac: "I believe in learning and questions."
Adam: "Debate and politics will be forbidden on school grounds."
Noah: "We need to keep this going."
Miss Sarah: "You have done something rare."
Samira: "I do not agree with all this liberal chatter, but I respect the courage it takes to speak."
After your partner speaks, report what they said. For example: Isaac said that he believed in learning and questions.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 4: Mini-Debate
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Choose one of the following topics and prepare a short argument (one to two minutes). Use discourse markers (well, basically, in fact, actually, for example) to connect your ideas. Then present your argument to a partner or your AI Tutor.
Silence is a more powerful form of protest than shouting.
Young people have the right to discuss politics at school.
One person can change the direction of a whole community.
It is better to follow rules even when you disagree with them.
Ask Your AI Tutor
Try asking: Can you be my debate partner? I want to argue that silence is a powerful form of protest. Please argue the other side.
Writing Practice
Writing allows you to bring together everything you have learned -- vocabulary, grammar, ideas, and structure. The activities below will guide you through different types of writing, from short exercises to a full narrative essay.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 5: Sentence Transformation
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Rewrite the following sentences using the grammar structure indicated in brackets. Write your answers in your notebook.
"I am happy with my essay," said Isaac. (Change to reported speech.)
The students played football. Isaac wrote an essay. (Combine using Past Continuous and Past Simple.)
Isaac finished the essay. The headmaster called him. (Rewrite using Past Perfect to show which happened first.)
She taught herself to speak three languages. (Identify the reflexive pronoun and explain its use.)
In those days, silence was enforced through rules and fear. (Identify the time phrase and explain what it signals.)
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 6: Paragraph Writing
activities.instructionsAndItems: Write a paragraph of 80 to 100 words describing a time when you or someone you know had to be brave. Use at least three vocabulary words from this unit and at least one example each of the Past Simple, Past Continuous, and Past Perfect tenses. Begin your paragraph with a time phrase such as "In those days" or "At the time."
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

assignments.title: Historical Figures and Legacy evidence portfolio
assignments.instructions: Submit Writing 1: , an annotated source comparison and a recording of Speaking 1: Story Retelling. Improve all pieces using the Grade 8 rubrics.
assignments.sourceFile: Derived from unit writing and speaking tasks

quizzes.question: What does '1. benign' mean?
quizzes.options: A person's usual mood or way of thinking. | A problem or fault in something that makes it not work properly. | Done according to a plan or system; organised and methodical. | Kind and gentle; not harmful or dangerous.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Kind and gentle; not harmful or dangerous.
quizzes.explanation: The teacher's benign smile made the students feel comfortable in class.

quizzes.question: What does '2. reprieve' mean?
quizzes.options: A hidden store of things, often used to keep items safe. | A person's usual mood or way of thinking. | A temporary break from something difficult or unpleasant. | Done according to a plan or system; organised and methodical.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A temporary break from something difficult or unpleasant.
quizzes.explanation: After studying hard for exams, I took a reprieve by playing video games for an hour.

quizzes.question: What does '3. defect' mean?
quizzes.options: A hidden store of things, often used to keep items safe. | A problem or fault in something that makes it not work properly. | Done according to a plan or system; organised and methodical. | Requiring a lot of effort and energy.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A problem or fault in something that makes it not work properly.
quizzes.explanation: The toy had a defect, so it did not work when we tried to use it.

quizzes.question: What does '4. disposition' mean?
quizzes.options: A hidden store of things, often used to keep items safe. | A person's usual mood or way of thinking. | Requiring a lot of effort and energy. | The final part of something where you summarise or decide what it means.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A person's usual mood or way of thinking.
quizzes.explanation: Her cheerful disposition makes everyone around her feel happy.

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Rebellion of Silence, part 1': But most saw him as harmless -- a _____ shadow in the back row.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: But most saw him as harmless -- a benign shadow in the back row.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Rebellion of Silence, part 2': It is sometimes the _____ in freedom." He went on, telling the story -- not by name, but clearly -- of what had happened.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: It is sometimes the defect in freedom." He went on, telling the story -- not by name, but clearly -- of what had happened.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Rebellion of Silence, part 1': Among the Form 4 students was Isaac, a boy with a quiet _____ and a habit of observing more than speaking.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: Among the Form 4 students was Isaac, a boy with a quiet disposition and a habit of observing more than speaking.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Rebellion of Silence, part 1': They were sharing a _____ of newspapers and pamphlets hidden in a biscuit tin.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: They were sharing a cache of newspapers and pamphlets hidden in a biscuit tin.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Historical Figures and Legacy: source text 1': Identify and use verbs followed by prepositions such as insist on, believe in, _____ between, and implore for.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: Identify and use verbs followed by prepositions such as insist on, believe in, waver between, and implore for.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Historical Figures and Legacy: source text 1': Recognise and use common phrasal verbs including get on with, get into trouble, _____ away, and roil over.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: Recognise and use common phrasal verbs including get on with, get into trouble, ebb away, and roil over.

teacherNotes.note: Model disciplinary language aloud, teach annotation and source checking explicitly, use mixed-response modes, and give specific feedback on evidence, cohesion, viewpoint and vocabulary precision while increasing learner independence.

Kind and gentle; not harmful or dangerous. The teacher's benign smile made the students feel comfortable in class.
A temporary break from something difficult or unpleasant. After studying hard for exams, I took a reprieve by playing video games for an hour.
A problem or fault in something that makes it not work properly. The toy had a defect, so it did not work when we tried to use it.
A person's usual mood or way of thinking. Her cheerful disposition makes everyone around her feel happy.
Done according to a plan or system; organised and methodical. She used a systematic approach to solve the maths problems step by step.
A hidden store of things, often used to keep items safe. The hikers found a cache of supplies hidden in the forest.
Requiring a lot of effort and energy. Running a marathon is a strenuous activity that takes a lot of practice.
The final part of something where you summarise or decide what it means. At the end of the story, the conclusion helped me understand the main message.
To be unsure about something or to change your mind. She started to waver between choosing chocolate or vanilla ice cream.
To decrease or become less, especially in strength or intensity. As the tide began to ebb, the water slowly moved away from the shore.
